title: COIVis : Eye-tracking-based Visual Exploration of Concept Learning in MOOC Videos
authors: Zhiguang Zhou, Ruiqi Yu, Yuming Ma, Hao Ni, Guojun Li, Li Ye, Xiaoying Wang, Yize Li, Yigang Wang, Yong Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: Massive Open Online Courses (MOOCs) make high-quality instruction accessible. However, the lack of face-to-face interaction makes it difficult for instructors to obtain feedback on learners' performance and provide more effective instructional guidance. Traditional analytical approaches, such as clickstream logs or quiz scores, capture only coarse-grained learning outcomes and offer limited insight into learners' moment-to-moment cognitive states. In this study, we propose COIVis, an eye-tracking-based visual analytics system that supports concept-level exploration of learning processes in MOOC videos. COIVis first extracts course concepts from multimodal video content and aligns them with the temporal structure and screen space of the lecture, defining Concepts of Interest (COIs), which anchor abstract concepts to specific spatiotemporal regions. Learners' gaze trajectories are transformed into COI sequences, and five interpretable learner-state features—Attention, Cognitive Load, Interest, Preference, and Synchronicity—are computed at the COI level based on eye tracking metrics. Building on these representations, COIVis provides a narrative, multi-view visualization enabling instructors to move from cohort-level overviews to individual learning paths, quickly locate problematic concepts, and compare diverse learning strategies. We evaluate COIVis through two case studies and in-depth user-feedback interviews. The results demonstrate that COIVis effectively provides instructors with valuable insights into the consistency and anomalies of learners' learning patterns, thereby supporting timely and personalized interventions for learners and optimizing instructional design.

title: An Intelligent Inversion Method to Identify Railway Ballast Gradation Parameters Using Electromagnetic Wave Reflection
authors: Hong-Jin Hu, Zheng-Quan Yang, Guang-Zhong Cao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Magnetics
abstract: Identifying ballast gradation parameters is crucial for ensuring the operational safety of railways. However, conventional railway ballast assessment methods that are based on mechanical sieving are destructive, time-consuming, and highly dependent on manual intervention, which limits their applicability for in situ and large-scale inspections. For the direct identification of ballast gradation parameters, an intelligent inversion method is proposed in this paper. First, a three-dimensional stochastic geometric model of railway ballast is established, in which the particle size distributions are controlled according to target gradation parameters. On the basis of this model, finite-difference time-domain (FDTD) simulations are performed using gprMax to generate ground penetrating radar (GPR) B-scan reflection responses that correspond to various ballast gradation conditions. A convolutional neural network for learning the nonlinear mapping between electromagnetic wave reflection responses and ballast gradation parameters is then developed. The results demonstrate that a reliable mapping between the GPR electromagnetic wave reflections and ballast gradation parameters can be established.

title: Hierarchical Path Planning and Task Offloading for UAV-Assisted MEC Systems
authors: Zihan Zheng, Ke He, Le He, Lisheng Fan, George K. Karagiannidis, Symeon Chatzinotas
publish: IEEE Transactions on Vehicular Technology
abstract: In this paper, we investigate the joint path planning and task offloading problem in UAV-assisted mobile edge computing (MEC) systems with multiple hotspots and priority-aware service requirements, under mission time and energy constraints. While the existing deep reinforcement learning (DRL) based approaches have recently shown great potentials in addressing the joint optimization problem, they often suffer from the curse of dimensionality and training instability as the system scales, due to the tightly-coupled decision space. To address these issues, we devise a reformulation of the joint optimization problem as an iterative solution refinement process inspired by the hierarchical reinforcement learning. Based on the reformulation, we propose a novel pivot-guided hierarchical path planning and task offloading (HPPTO) algorithm, where a meta-controller identifies a pivot substructure in the current plan, while a low-level controller refines it via a series of fine-grained local edits to jointly improve the flight plan and offloading decisions. This hierarchical structure transforms the exponential per-decision branching factor into linear branching factors, therefore significantly reducing the search complexity and improving scalability. Our extensive simulation results demonstrate that HPPTO consistently outperforms state-of-the-art DRL baselines, showing significantly improved scalability as the system scales up.

title: Direct Estimation of P- and S-Wave Impedance Parameters based on Pre-stack Reflection Coefficients
authors: Zhen Peng, Danping Cao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: The longitudinal and transverse wave impedance is a core parameter for describing the characteristics of reservoirs, and there is the most direct physical relationship between it and the seismic reflection coefficient. However, most of the existing impedance inversion methods rely on prior constraints such as regularization to improve the ill-posed, which makes the inversion process subject to many additional factors and difficult to achieve direct and stable impedance prediction. Therefore, this paper proposes an inversion method for directly estimating the impedance of the longitudinal and transverse waves based on the pre-stack reflection coefficient to achieve a more direct and stable impedance estimation. By introducing Sparse Incremental Selection and Shrinkage Operator (SISSO) regression algorithm, the explicit mathematical expressions between P-wave impedance, S-wave impedance, density and reflection coefficient is directly established, so as to achieve more direct and stable pre-stack parameter estimation without relying on regularization constraints. Experimental results based on single-well synthetic record, Marmousi model and field seismic data show that, compared with the traditional AVO inversion method, the proposed method effectively eliminates the reliance on prior constraints while maintaining the accuracy and stability of parameter estimation, providing a new approach to improve the stability and reliability of pre-stack AVO parameter prediction.

title: Overcoming Resistance to Change for Sustainable ICT Integration in Logistics: From Case Studies to Best Practices_supp1-3693049.docx
authors: Ahmed Sahraoui
publish: N/A
abstract: In the supply chains' context, the integration of Information and Communication Technology (ICT) has the potential to significantly enhance operational performance. However, resistance to change remains a major barrier to successful ICT implementation. This study investigates how resistance manifests and affects the adoption process of an ICT solution within a large logistics organisation in Le Havre, France. A mixed-methods approach combining participant observation, semi-structured interviews and survey analysis is employed to identify behavioural, organisational and procedural obstacles. Findings reveal that inadequate communication, insufficient training and workforce turnover may critically undermine ICT project success. Moreover, a complementary and exploratory statistical analysis based on Partial Least Squares and robustness checks suggests that satisfaction, perceived added value and software familiarisation are the most influential factors affecting adoption success, while ease of use and comfort appear to be not significant. These findings indicate the importance of user engagement and expectations in the performance of ICT tools and provide insights for managing resistance and aligning technology initiatives with organisational capabilities.

title: Learning Spatiotemporal Dependencies for Tropical Cyclone Intensity Forecasting with Multi-Source Data
authors: Lei Liu, Hongwei Zhao, Xinyu Wang, Kang Chen, Xiaoning Yu, Tao Han, Bin Li, Lei Bai
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Accurate prediction of tropical cyclone (TC) intensity is essential for effective disaster mitigation. Existing methods mainly rely on limited spatiotemporal information from ERA5 and overlook the spatiotemporal dependencies between historical intensity sequences and spatial environmental features, which restricts their ability to capture the full spatial and temporal patterns needed for intensity forecasting. To address these limitations, we propose the Multi-source Multi-Scale Constrained AutoRegressive (MSCAR) model, the first approach to combine spatiotemporal dependency modeling with large-scale multi-source datasets for autoregressive prediction of global TC intensity. In addition, to fill the gap of a comprehensive dataset covering diverse spatial variables, we introduce the Multi-Source Satellite and ERA5-based Tropical Cyclone Dataset (MSETCD), currently the longest and most complete global dataset for TCs. Experiments show that MSCAR outperforms existing methods, reducing forecast errors globally and regionally by up to 9.52% and 6.74%, respectively. The dataset and source code are openly available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/USTC-AI4EEE/MSCAR</uri>

title: Frequency-Domain Scalar Message Passing Based Joint Target Detection and Localization for Distributed Passive MIMO Radar
authors: Bin Li, Hailong Kang, Jun Li, Qinghua Guo
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: Joint target detection and localization using distributed passive MIMO radar has received much attention. However, existing methods are typically computationally expensive and exhibit severe performance degradation under the strong direct-path interference (DPI) and noise variance uncertainty. In this work, we address the challenges by proposing a frequency-domain scalar message passing based joint target detection and localization method. Firstly, we formulate the joint target detection and localization problem in a probabilistic way, and particularly a frequency-domain model is used, which enables low-complexity implementation. Then, a factor graph approach is proposed to efficiently solve the problem, where we take advantage of the frequency-domain model to develop a hybrid scalar message passing algorithm, leading to significant computational complexity reduction. Secondly, to deal with the unknown noise precision (i.e., the reciprocal of noise variance), its estimation is embedded in the proposed algorithm, allowing online noise precision estimation, i.e., the noise variance estimation is simultaneously performed with target detection and localization. Moreover, we prove that the proposed method exhibits the constant false alarm rate (CFAR) property against the unknown noise variances and DPI. Extensive simulation results validate the CFAR property and demonstrate the superior performance of the proposed method compared to state-of-the-art ones, while with lower computational complexity.

title: Robust Adversarial Defense for Deep Learning-Based Beamforming in Next-Generation Wireless Networks: Knowledge Distillation Meets TRADES
authors: Ahmed Abd El Moaty Mohamed Gouda, Ehab K. I. Hamad, Mohamed A. Ismeil, M. Mourad Mabrook, A. A. Donkol
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Deep learning has become a powerful tool for beamforming optimization in next-generation wireless networks, where it often outperforms traditional methods in massive MIMO systems. However, neural networks remain vulnerable to adversarial attacks, and even small perturbations can cause major performance degradation in real-time communication systems. In this work, we propose a defense framework that combines Knowledge Distillation (KD) with TRadeoff-inspired Adversarial defense via Surrogate-loss minimization (TRADES) to improve the robustness of deep learning-based beamforming. The framework is evaluated on three realistic DeepMIMO scenarios: outdoor urban (O1), outdoor aerial drone (O1 Drone), and indoor conference room (I3). The results show that KD+TRADES maintains achievable rates of 2.5-3.1 bps/Hz under strong attacks up to ε = 0.5. Undefended teacher models, by contrast, experience catastrophic failure under the same conditions. while undefended models degrade severely. The proposed method also limits MSE to below 0.07 across all tested perturbation budgets, whereas undefended models exceed 1.6 at high epsilon values. Interestingly, the KD student model achieves the lowest clean data MSE in all scenarios (0.0173-0.0189), beating the teacher baseline. Although KD+TRADES introduces a modest clean-data MSE increase of 18–23%, it provides substantially stronger robustness across FGSM, PGD-20, BIM, and MIM attacks. These results suggest that combining knowledge distillation with adversarial training offers a practical defense for securing deep learning-based beamforming in wireless systems.

title: Diffusion Bridge-Based Joint Estimation and Detection Design for Superimposed-Pilot OFDM Receiver
authors: Rui Zeng, Jingbo Tan, Xu Shi, Jintao Wang, Yifeng Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Broadcasting
abstract: Superimposed-pilot (SP) transmission improves spectral efficiency but poses significant challenges for receiver design due to pilot–data interference, which conventional algorithms struggle to address effectively under fast-fading conditions. To address this issue, this paper proposes a diffusion bridge–based receiver for joint channel estimation and data detection. By formulating the received signals as one endpoint distribution and the transmitted data together with the channel impulse response as the other endpoint distribution, the conditional probability path between them is learned while implicitly exploiting OFDM structural constraints. To enable more stable and efficient training, the network is designed to directly predict the clean output, and the corresponding flow-velocity loss function for data detection is derived. Furthermore, a two-stage factor scheduler is introduced to balance the training objectives of channel estimation and data detection. Simulation results show that the proposed method achieves lower channel estimation mean square error (MSE) and data detection bit error rate (BER) than existing schemes, while exhibits sufficient generalization ability at higher subcarriers and modulation orders, demonstrating its effectiveness as a data-driven solution for reliable communication in dynamic environments.

title: Cross-Frequency Feature Learning for Dual-Band Radar HRRP Target Recognition
authors: Wei Yang, Weijia Wang, Yanhua Wang, Liang Zhang, Xin Zhang, Yuqi Han, Yang Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: Radar ground target recognition using high-resolution range profile (HRRP) has attracted considerable attention recently. Existing methods primarily focus on single-band data, yet the HRRP of targets with similar shape structures are highly similar, making this a challenging task in complex scenarios. To address this, we use dual-band HRRPs to exploit target's scattering variations across frequencies, thereby improving recognition. In the paper, a cross-frequency feature learning (CFFL) method is proposed to extract inter-frequency discriminative features via both handcrafted and deep-learning perspectives. The handcrafted features are extracted at raw HRRP levels and their single-band feature level. The resulting heterogeneous feature sets are optimized using sparse coding and coding rate divergence maximization. For deep features, wavelet packet decomposition is integrated into a neural network to enable multi-scale feature extraction. Attention mechanism is employed to characterize the wavelet domain differences between the dual-band data, with training guided by a difference search loss function. Frequency interaction further enhances the deep feature representations. Finally, deep discriminant correlation analysis is applied to jointly optimize both feature types, preserving optimal discriminative information. Extensive experiments on three datasets were conducted to verify the proposed method's effectiveness.

title: Efficient Ambiguity Resolution for BDS-3/INS positioning with Multi-frequency Measurements
authors: Kai Xiao, Yuzhe Wang, Runyi Shi, Lundong Zhang, Wanli Li, Jiansheng Li, Guorui Xiao, Peiyuan Zhou
publish: IEEE Transactions on Vehicular Technology
abstract: The global BeiDou navigation system/Inertial Navigation System (BDS-3/INS) has been widely applied. Ambiguity resolution (AR) is critical for achieving centimeter-level positioning accuracy for BDS-3/INS. This study proposes a novel inertial aided multi-frequency ambiguity resolution method (I-MAR), which addresses challenges in reliability and efficiency when existing methods are used for BDS-3/INS. First, the state and measurement equations for BDS-3/INS are introduced. Then, the mathematical model of I-MAR was established, based on which optimal measurement combinations for triple-, quad-, and five-frequency scenarios were investigated. The performance of I-MAR was compared with typical methods such as INS-aided LAMBDA and MCAR through vehicle test data. The AR correct rate of I-MAR is comparable to INS-aided LAMBDA but with significantly higher efficiency. Moreover, the BDS-3 signal loss-recovery scenario was simulated, and instantaneous single-epoch ambiguity recovery was realized after a signal loss of up to 40 s. Finally, the accuracy of position, velocity, and attitude was assessed; the position error in the east, north, and up directions were 0.028m, 0.02m, and 0.035m, respectively. This further verifies the suitability and superiority of the I-MAR when applied to BDS-3/INS.

title: GNSS-Spoofing Detection via Machine Learning with In-Domain and Cross-Domain Testing and Open-Access I/Q Data
authors: Nadir Khan, Leatile Marata, Jari Nurmi, Elena Simona Lohan
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Global Navigation Satellite Systems (GNSS) are essential for applications that rely on positioning, velocity, and timing (PVT) information. Unfortunately, a surge in interferences in GNSS frequency bands causes severe security risks for various navigation-based applications. Traditional signal-processing techniques cannot generalize well to different interference types, therefore, Radio Frequency Fingerprinting (RFF) methods using sophisticated Machine Learning (ML) methods are used to capture complex patterns in GNSS signals. However, ML-based spoofing detection studies have relied primarily on the testing of the ML models with the same type of spoofing signals as those used in the training phase (the in-domain testing). This is a strong limitation of ML-based RFF, as real-time spoofers are highly variable and time-varying. Therefore, the ability of an ML algorithm to provide cross-domain generalization across unseen datasets is crucial. To this end, our paper presents a comprehensive analysis of achievable spoofing-detection accuracies using four ML models and compares the in-domain with cross-domain testing accuracies. We used multiple scenarios from two large open-access GNSS spoofing repositories: the Texas Spoofing Test Battery (TEXBAT) and the Oakridge Spoofing Test Battery (OAKBAT). For in-domain testing with mixed sample split, all ML models achieve an average classification accuracy greater than 90%. The accuracy for PRN-level becomes slightly lower, with around 80% in GPS L1 and around 75% in Galileo E1. Finally, the average accuracy in the cross-testing approach drops to 66% or less, highlighting the need for more advanced ML models to achieve higher generalization capabilities under complex spoofing conditions.

title: Predicting Tactile Sensory Outcome of Physical Human-Robot Interaction through Embodied Learning Strategy_supp2-3692059.mp4
authors: Yuxi Lu
publish: N/A
abstract: Estimation of robotic dynamic states in physical human-robot interaction (pHRI) is crucial for robots to handle real-world uncertainties. Since wearable tactile sensor arrays have been increasingly applied in robots, the tactile sensory outcomes (TSOs) during pHRIs would provide practical and ideal information about dynamic states of a robot as responses of its actions. In this work, an embodied learning strategy is introduced to promote the formation of a deep learning-based embodied haptic model (DL-EH), enabling robots to implicitly learn their tactile sensorimotor dynamics in pHRI. Functionally, DL-EH adopts a spatial-temporal assist module (embodied haptic embedding) to assist a compact forward predictor (multimodal mapping and sensory prediction) in learning TSO prediction. Then, it can realize an end-to-end real-time TSO prediction of robot actions in pHRI based on current tactile signals without historical sequences. In real-world experiments, the effectiveness of DL-EH is demonstrated, especially in long-term TSO predictions. The generalization test is performed to validate DL-EH's generality in new pHRI scenarios. Finally, a robotic framework via the proposed method is proposed for proof-of-concept in the pHRI application.

title: MalElves: Reinforcement Learning-Driven Adversarial Example Generation for Evading Cross-Platform ELF Malware Detection
authors: Zhangbo Long, Letian Sha, Yan Lin, Peijie Sun, Haiping Huang, Fu Xiao, Zhiquan Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Software Engineering
abstract: Adversarial Example (AE) generation is a key instrument for stress-testing and hardening malware detectors, yet most existing techniques target Portable Executable (PE) files and do not transfer cleanly to Executable and Linkable Format (ELF) binaries prevalent in Internet of Things (IoT) environments. We propose <monospace xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">MalElves</monospace>, a reinforcement learning-driven AE generation framework for cross-platform ELF malware. <monospace xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">MalElves</monospace> makes three core technical contributions. First, a code-data-aware manipulation framework unifies obfuscation and rewriting across ARM, ×86, and ×64 architectures while preserving functionality. Second, a sample-efficient state design reduces 2,350 raw ELF features to a compact 21-dimensional input. Third, a shaped multi-detector reward uses fully disclosed PPO settings for full reproducibility. We evaluate <monospace xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">MalElves</monospace> on 161,414 malware samples and 74,260 benign samples. We test against four static detectors and a behavioral-ensemble stress test. The method achieves average ASRs of 89.9%, 85.3%, 63.8%, 60.6%, and 24.7% across detectors. The overall average ASR reaches 64.8%. Each successful evasion requires 2.24 interaction rounds on average.

title: Disturbance Perception and Accurate Modeling of Magnetic Suspension Flywheel Battery Based on Dynamic Coupling Preview Matrices
authors: Weiyu Zhang, Yao Wu
publish: IEEE/ASME Transactions on Mechatronics
abstract: The magnetic suspension flywheel battery is a multisubsystem integrated entity, with multiple inherent cross-coupling effects within it. Under complex road conditions, these effects can become unknown disturbances that affect model parameters and reduce control accuracy. Due to the complex and variable nature of the coupling effects, it is difficult to completely decouple them. Therefore, in contrast to the traditional approach of considering them as disturbances that need to be suppressed or decoupled, the coupling effects disturbances are treated as effective modeling information and the disturbance perception and accurate modeling method based on the dynamic coupling preview matrices are proposed in this article. Through the analysis of coupling mechanism, the influence of the disturbance of the multiphysics fields coupling effects on the parameters of the flywheel battery model is perceived, and the dynamic coupling preview matrices that characterizes the variation of parameters under disturbance is proposed in this article. Then, based on preview matrices, an accurate suspension force model that can anticipate the influence of the coupled effect disturbance and update the parameters according to the working conditions is established to improve the control accuracy of the system. Finally, the results of the validity and performance experiments verify the accuracy of the proposed model and the stability of the overall system constructed based on this model.

title: Deep Spatio-Temporal and Frequency Guided Fusion Network for Event-to-Video Reconstruction
authors: Ramna Maqsood, Paulo Nunes, Caroline Conti, Luís Ducla Soares
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Signal Processing
abstract: Event-to-video (E2V) reconstruction has gained significant attention recently for its advantages in enabling high dynamic range and fast motion capture capabilities. However, event data encodes only relative brightness changes, lacking the absolute intensity information necessary for accurate reconstruction. Recent methods incorporate previously reconstructed images to provide intensity references but process them in the spatial domain where low- and high-frequency components are highly coupled. This spatial processing typically leads to the degradation of fine details and introduces artifacts such as over-smoothing, blurring and low contrast reconstruction. To address this, we propose a deep spatio-temporal and frequency guided fusion network for E2V reconstruction (DSTFN-E2V), featuring a dual-path architecture with two key components: i) a prior frequency decomposition module (PFDM), and ii) a spatio-temporal event-driven feature extraction module (STEM). The PFDM decouples low- and high-frequency information from previously reconstructed images and current event voxel grid via a 2D discrete wavelet transform, processing the low-frequency subband through residual blocks to preserve structural coherence and intensity references, while an edge-detail refinement module (ERM) enhances edge and texture details from high-frequency subbands. The frequency-specific features from PFDM and the spatio-temporal features from STEM are then integrated through the proposed event-image fusion blocks (EIFBs) that apply cross-attention across three encoder stages, enabling simultaneous structural preservation and detail recovery. Experiments on four real-world datasets demonstrate that DSTFN-E2V achieves state-of-the-art results with 12% SSIM improvements while being 50% faster than recent attention-based methods, with superior edge fidelity and reduced artifacts.

title: Curiosity-Driven Reinforcement Learning for Autonomous Navigation in Magnetically Controlled Capsule Endoscopy
authors: Soheil Dehghani, Parastoo Mohsenipour, Farhan Pahlevani Kiya, Mahshad Mohammadi, Raha Farahani, Borhan Beigzadeh
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Gastrointestinal disorders affect approximately 40% of the global population, presenting diagnostic challenges that conventional endoscopic procedures struggle to address due to invasiveness and miss rates ranging from 6% to 28%. While magnetically controlled capsule endoscopy (MCCE) offers a minimally invasive alternative, achieving comprehensive anatomical coverage remains challenging due to sample inefficiency and limited robustness in reinforcement learning-based navigation systems. This paper introduces Adaptive Spectral Exploration - Proximal Policy Optimization (ASE-PPO), a curiosity-driven reinforcement learning framework combining Random Network Distillation (RND) with spectral parameter space noise for autonomous gastric navigation. The primary novelty lies in the synergistic integration of three contributions: spectrally structured colored noise enabling coherent directional exploration, direct coupling between RND-derived novelty and parameter perturbation amplitude, and an O(1) incremental normalization framework for scalable deployment. Validation in VR-Caps demonstrates that ASE-PPO achieves 95.4% gastric coverage at 120 seconds — representing 4.15–6.17% improvements over baseline PPO — with ablation studies confirming RND’s contribution beyond spectral noise alone. ASE-PPO additionally demonstrates 18.6% standard deviation reduction and 21.8% coefficient of variation improvement, with statistically significant coverage gains at 120 seconds (p = 0.035), establishing curiosity-driven reinforcement learning with structured exploration as a promising paradigm for autonomous gastrointestinal examination systems.

title: On the Convergence of the Fast Griffin-Lim Algorithm
authors: Alessandro Ilic Mezza, Massimo Cicognani, Roberto Leone Cicognani, Alberto Bernardini
publish: IEEE Signal Processing Letters
abstract: The Fast Griffin-Lim Algorithm (FGLA) is one of the most widely used iterative methods for phase reconstruction and signal estimation from modified short-time Fourier transforms. Unlike the classic Griffin-Lim Algorithm (GLA), however, the convergence of FGLA has not yet been fully proved, with existing theoretical guarantees holding only for momenta significantly smaller than those found to perform best in practice. In this letter, we build upon the appendix of the original paper by Griffin and Lim and formulate GLA as a gradient descent algorithm. From this, we show that FGLA corresponds to an accelerated gradient descent method with constant momentum. We then derive a sufficient condition ensuring convergence over a broad range of momenta and formulate a criterion to assess its validity in numerical experiments, which we find is typically satisfied within the first few iterations.

title: An LMI Approach to Admissibility Analysis and H ∞ Observer-Based Control Design with Fault Estimation for Singular Fractional-Order Systems
authors: Arunagirinathan Srinivasan, PooGyeon Park, David Banjerdpongchai
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: This paper develops an improved theoretical framework for admissibility analysis and H∞ observer design of a singular fractional-order systems (SFOSs) in the presence of disturbances and faults. A novel bounded real lemma (BRL) is first established in terms of real-valued linear matrix inequalities (LMIs). Unlike previous BRL conditions for SFOSs, the proposed formulation eliminates equality constraints and avoids complex-valued free variables by constructing an equivalent real-domain representation. This leads to a convex optimization condition that guarantees admissibility and prescribed H∞ performance simultaneously, while significantly reducing conservatism. Building upon the derived BRL, both state-feedback control and observer-based control synthesis conditions are developed within a unified LMI framework, enabling simultaneous stabilization and robust performance enforcement for the SFOSs. An observer-based state and fault estimation scheme is then formulated accordingly. The introduction of additional decision matrices enlarges the feasible solution set and enables attenuation level optimization. A systematic minimization procedure for the disturbance attenuation index is established, providing improved robustness guarantees. Comparative numerical results confirm that the proposed theoretical conditions yield a significantly reduced attenuation gain and enhanced estimation performance relative to previous methods, thereby demonstrating the effectiveness and reduced conservatism of the improved theory.

title: Rotor Speed Estimation of Synchronous Generators Based on PMU Data and Tuning of Linearized Swing Equation Coefficients
authors: Thales Pedro S. Esperança, Tatiane Cristina C. Fernandes, Edson L. Geraldi, Guilherme G. Lage, Gustavo P. Eustáquio, Rodrigo A. Ramos
publish: IEEE Open Access Journal of Power and Energy
abstract: The rotor speed of Synchronous Generators (SGs) is a fundamental dynamic state that reflects the stored kinetic energy and the generator’s inertial response to system disturbances. Accurate rotor-speed estimation enables the detection of poorly damped electromechanical oscillations that may lead to instability. However, precise assessment of SG dynamics is often hindered by model inaccuracies and parametric uncertainties. With the increasing deployment of Phasor Measurement Units (PMUs), novel approaches have emerged to enhance post-event system diagnosis, particularly in low-inertia systems. This paper proposes a two-stage framework for estimating the rotor speed of SGs by tuning the coefficients of the linearized swing equation using data from PMUs, thus eliminating the need for the complete Electric Power System (EPS) model. In the first stage, the Morris sensitivity method identifies the most influential coefficients to refine the parameter-estimation domain. In the second stage, an Entropy-Based Genetic Algorithm (EB-GA) refines these coefficients by minimizing the mean squared error between measured EPS dynamics and the model response. Simulation results on the IEEE 68-bus system demonstrate that the proposed framework effectively captures SG rotor-speed dynamics, even under small perturbations and noisy PMU data. The EB-GA achieves more precise parameter tuning than conventional genetic algorithms, improving the representation of SG dynamics for post-event analysis and subsequent controller adjustment in EPSs.

title: Optimal Stealthy Attacks Under Periodic Detection in Cyber-Physical Systems With Round-Robin Protocol
authors: Jian-Xing Wang, Xiao-Guang Zhang, Xiu-Xiu Ren, Peng Gao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Informatics
abstract: This article focuses on the design of an optimal stealthy attack strategy for cyber-physical systems operating under the round-robin protocol (RRP) in a more realistic setting, where the control period and the detection period are mismatched. The objective is to maximize the attack impact while maintaining stealthiness. In contrast to existing RRP-based attack strategies that assume coincident control and detection periods, the synchronous control-detection assumption is removed, and a novel protocol-aware optimal attack strategy is developed to coordinate actions across detection and nondetection instants. The proposed strategy effectively addresses the challenge posed by the RRP, under which only partial transmitted data can be manipulated at each time step. The optimal attack parameters are obtained by solving a convex optimization problem, where closed-form expressions are derived, avoiding numerical iterations and inequality-based extremum methods required by existing approaches. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed strategy induces more severe degradation in remote state estimation performance and validate the theoretical analysis.

title: Design and Implementation of Cloud-Ground-End Collaborative Zero-Trust Security Architecture for Industrial Drones
authors: Chen Yuan, Haifeng Shan, Mei Chen, Zhanfeng Wang
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Advanced Algorithms and Control Engineering (ICAACE)
abstract: Faced with the security challenges of industrial drones in the “cloud-ground-end” dynamic collaborative operation, traditional static boundary protection is ineffective, leading to out-of-control access and data leakage. This paper designs and implements a collaborative zero-trust security architecture. First, an overall architecture with dynamic trust assessment as the core and separation of policy decision-making and execution was built. Then, a real-time trust quantification model and dynamic access control strategy that integrated multi-dimensional contextual information were designed. Then, a lightweight security module suitable for resource-constrained terminals was developed. Finally, a policy synchronization and trust chain transfer mechanism that supports seamless cross-domain switching was implemented. Experiments show that this architecture can effectively trigger active blocking (e.g., 8 blocks/600 seconds), control performance overhead (e.g., CPU increase of approximately 52%), and ensure business continuity (cross-domain interruption of approximately 158ms). The conclusion confirms that this architecture provides a dynamic and endogenous safety solution for industrial drone operations in an open environment.

title: IoT-Integrated Smart Monitoring of Buck-Boost Converter Performance in Multi-Voltage Battery Systems
authors: G. Rajasekar, N V Kishore Kumar, K. Pradeepa, R. Priyadharshini, G. Janani, A. Naveena Sree
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Trends in Material Science and Inventive Materials (ICTMIM)
abstract: The proliferation of multi-voltage battery systems in portable electronics, electric mobility and smart energy platforms has generated an increased demand for real-time monitoring and efficient voltage regulation capabilities inside power converters. Therefore, this work presents an Internet of Things (IoT) based smart monitoring architecture for a Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) driven buck-boost converter connected to a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 2 V} \boldsymbol{/} \mathbf{2 4 V} \boldsymbol{/} \mathbf{4 8 V}$</tex> battery system, with a focus on converter operation characterised by thermal deviation and mode-wise performance evaluation. The research gap being filled is the unique combination of converter operation analysis coupled with IoT-based performance observation in diverse application; a multi-level battery scenario wherein stable voltage conversion and current control are required while also maintaining thermal reliability under varying input conditions. Duty-cycle, switching-frequency, buck, boost and buck-boost conversion relations are used to develop the mathematical modelling. The results detailed indicate that for the selected battery inputs, buck-mode output voltages of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$7.2 ~\mathrm{V}, 14.4 ~\mathrm{V}$</tex> and 28.8 V are achieved alongside boost-mode output voltages of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$30 ~\mathrm{V}, 60 ~\mathrm{V}$</tex> and 120 V. The respective output currents in buck mode are <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.72 ~\mathrm{A}, 1.44$</tex> A and 2.88 A. While boost-mode currents are <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1.5 ~\mathrm{A}, 3.0 ~\mathrm{A}$</tex> and 6.0 A, thermal profiling shows a variation from 34° C to 57° C across the range of operation, indicating the practical necessity for some spot thermal control in intelligent converter applications.

title: Design of a Compact Diamond-Shaped MIMO Antenna with Enhanced Isolation Characteristics Using Hybrid DGS and EBG Techniques
authors: Abdulbari M. Almaghribi, Moustafa M. Abdalla, Albatool A. Elmezwghi
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Maghreb Meeting of the Conference on Sciences and Techniques of Automatic Control and Computer Engineering (MI-STA)
abstract: This paper presents a compact dual-port diamondshaped planar wideband MIMO antenna for millimeter-wave $\mathbf{5 G}$ applications operating over the $20-48 \mathrm{GHz}$ frequency range. A new hybrid decoupling approach combining a Defected Ground Structure (DGS) and Electromagnetic Band-Gap (EBG) elements is employed to effectively mitigate mutual coupling between antenna ports. The proposed antenna demonstrates excellent MIMO performance, particularly at 30 GHz, with isolation better than -30 dB and ultra-low envelope correlation coefficient (ECC) well below 0.0001, a channel capacity loss (CCL) below 0.15 bits/s/Hz, and a total active reflection coefficient (TARC) of -8.36 dB. The synergistic integration of DGS and EBG structures significantly enhances port isolation and diversity characteristics while maintaining wide impedance bandwidth. To ensure numerical reliability, the antenna performance is cross-validated using two independent full-wave electromagnetic solvers: Ansys HFSS and CST Microwave Studio. The results confirm that the proposed antenna is a strong candidate for compact, highperformance wideband 5G millimeter-wave wireless communication systems.

title: Research on Pipeline Risk Event Recognition Algorithm Based on CNN-Swin Transformer
authors: Jinlu Wang, Yingying Wang, Liang Zhu, Jinhuan Xu, Zhenbo Sun, Xingyu Li
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Advanced Algorithms and Control Engineering (ICAACE)
abstract: The monitoring of pipelines is of great significance for reducing safety hazards and improving economic benefits. To address the issue of low accuracy in the identification of pipeline risk events by distributed acoustic sensing (DAS) systems, an event recognition algorithm combining the convolutional neural network (CNN) and the Swin Transformer architecture is proposed. Firstly, the Swin Transformer is used to process the spatiotemporal graph, modeling the global relationship through its attention mechanism. Subsequently, the one-dimensional data is transformed into a two-dimensional time-frequency graph via the generalized S transform (GST), and then the time-frequency graph is processed by the CNN to extract time-frequency features. Finally, the multi-level features are fused and the pipeline risk events are accurately identified through a classifier. Experimental results show that the average recognition accuracy of the proposed algorithm reaches 95.20 %, which is an improvement over other algorithms, providing an effective means for pipeline monitoring.

title: Indian Currency Recognition with Audio Feedback for Visually Impaired People Using YOLOv5
authors: M. Malathi, A. Philomina Jenifer, N. Ashok Kumar, M.I. Anju, M. Bharathi, P. Nagarajan
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Trends in Material Science and Inventive Materials (ICTMIM)
abstract: One of the most important elements of financial independence for the blind and visually impaired is the ability to identify and differentiate between currency denominations rapidly. This relatively straightforward activity poses tremendous challenges, however, to the complete engagement of the blind and visually impaired in economic transactions. Equal access to money's acceptance is key to promoting inclusiveness and independence because money continues to be a basic aspect of everyday commerce. Since conventional means of determining currency often depend on touch characteristics or external aid, visually impaired individuals are less autonomous when dealing with money. This paper solves these issues by proposing a new solution that is particularly beneficial for visually impaired individuals: it employs the YOLOv5 algorithm to detect various denominations of currency and offer auditory feedback We propose a solution using computer vision and deep learning to develop real-time, accurate, and rapid answers to credit and banknote denomination detection problems using latest techniques. Our goal is to develop a robust system that is able to appropriately identify the different denominations of banknotes in various settings, to help address the challenge of banknote denomination detection under various climatic conditions This article describes how we set up, deployed and tested our YOLOv5 currency recognition system. The quality of this system has been determined through extensive testing with an accuracy of 96.4 %. As part of the effort to promote the independence of visually impaired persons, we aim to eliminate the barriers posed by cash transactions

title: AI Based Irrigation and Weather Forecasting System Using Lorawan
authors: G.Ravikumar Reddy, Bandari Manisha, Ramadevi Vemula
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Trends in Material Science and Inventive Materials (ICTMIM)
abstract: For Precision agriculture, the model developed in this research provides efficient method for field monitoring and automatic irrigation management, it uses LoRa technology, Arduino, Raspberry pi and cloud. By utilizing cutting-edge technology, a novel method for improving irrigation as developed. Plant parameters are measured using sensors, which are interfaced with an Arduino UNO to collect the data. Using LoRa technology the Sensor data is transferred to Raspberry Pi. integrating sensor data with meteorological information weather forecast model optimize irrigation. Sensors located near plants guarantee accurate data for watering plants individually. The proposed model focuses on precision forming and effective utilization of natural resources for harmony with nature.

title: Fault Diagnosis of TRD Machine Based on TimeGAN-LSTM-LightGBM
authors: Liang Zhao, Jingxi Cao
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Advanced Algorithms and Control Engineering (ICAACE)
abstract: The construction of the TRD (Trench Cutting Remixing Deep Wall) machine faces many challenges, such as complex working conditions and numerous disturbance factors. Its fault conditions often manifest as a combination of multiple operational parameters. Due to the requirement that frequent faults are not allowed during construction, fault samples are typically difficult to obtain, which poses challenges for training fault diagnosis models. Additionally, if faults and their types cannot be diagnosed promptly and accurately, significant property losses and casualties may occur. To address the scarcity of fault samples for the TRD machine and leverage the temporal characteristics of faults, a fault diagnosis method based on the TimeGAN-LSTM-LightGBM model is proposed. First, the collected raw time-series dataset is cleaned and used to train the TimeGAN model with three types of fault samples for sample expansion. Next, a three-layer stacked LSTM network is employed to extract temporal features. Finally, the generated samples are used to train the LightGBM model for fault diagnosis and performance metric analysis. An experimental study was conducted using the operational data from a selected TRD machine over the past year, following the above steps. The results demonstrate that the proposed model exhibits good diagnostic performance and can detect a higher proportion of fault samples, thereby verifying the effectiveness of the diagnosis method.

title: An Improved DEIM-Based Algorithm for X-Ray Prohibited Item Detection
authors: Xuemei Ni, Chunling Liu
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Advanced Algorithms and Control Engineering (ICAACE)
abstract: X-ray security inspection faces challenges of blurred boundaries, occlusion, and small objects. This paper proposes an improved DEIM-D-FINE-N algorithm for prohibited item detection. The MANet-LWGA backbone enhances boundary features via multi-path aggregation. The SPAE module decouples features for occlusion reasoning. FMFFNet integrates shallow features to boost small object detection. Experiments on SIXray show 95.5% mAP50 with 3.17M parameters and 129.3 FPS, outperforming mainstream detectors while meeting real-time requirements.

title: Robust Trajectory Tracking Control for Quadrotor UAV Based on Adaptive Dynamic Programming and Finite-Time Observer
authors: Yixiang Guo
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Advanced Algorithms and Control Engineering (ICAACE)
abstract: To address the trajectory tracking problem of quadrotor unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) under model parameter uncertainties (mass, moment of inertia, friction coefficients) and external time-varying disturbances, this paper proposes a robust control scheme that combines a Finite-Time Neural Network Observer with Adaptive Dynamic Programming. First, the nonlinear dynamic model of the quadrotor UAV is established and decoupled into position and attitude subsystems. Second, a finite-time neural network observer based on an integral sliding mode surface is designed for fast and accurate estimation of the lumped disturbances in the system. Subsequently, an approximate optimal controller is designed based on the ActorCritic architecture, utilizing neural networks to online approximate the solution of the Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman equation, achieving optimal tracking in unknown dynamic environments. Finally, MATLAB simulations verify the effectiveness and robustness of the proposed control strategy under complex disturbances.

title: CNC-MIND: An Explainable Multi-Task Digital Twin for CNC Machining
authors: Shakil Mohammad Nurus Sobha, Xiaobin Li, Islam Ariful, Fatemeh Bahmaninezhad
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Advanced Algorithms and Control Engineering (ICAACE)
abstract: Computer Numerical Control (CNC) machining generates rich multi-modal sensor data during operation, yet practical monitoring systems typically assess tool condition, process execution, and part quality in isolation. This separation limits diagnostic insight and reduces the effectiveness of digital twin-based decision support in production environments. This paper presents <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$C N C$</tex>-MIND, an explainable multi-task digital twin framework for CNC machining that jointly predicts tool condition, machining completion status, and post-process visual inspection outcomes from time-windowed sensor signals and process metadata. The framework employs a hybrid CNN-LSTM-DNN architecture to capture local signal patterns, longterm temporal dynamics, and operational context within a shared representation. To improve physical consistency, a physics-guided regularization term penalizes predictions that violate known relationships between vibration energy and tool degradation. Model transparency is achieved through integrated SHAP explanations, enabling both global feature attribution and instancelevel diagnostic reasoning. The proposed approach is evaluated on a real-world CNC milling dataset from the University of Michigan SMART Lab, demonstrating consistent performance gains over tuned DNN, CNN, and LSTM baselines across all tasks. CNC-MIND is further deployed within an interactive web-based digital twin platform, illustrating how multi-task predictions and explainability can be operationalized for human-in-the-loop CNC monitoring.

title: Hybrid RL-PID for Intelligent Lighting Control in Container-based Indoor Farming
authors: Albert, Good Fried Panggabean, Rahmad Romadhon, Endra Joelianto
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Technology, Informatics, Management, Engineering and Environment (TIME-E)
abstract: Container-based indoor farming presents a viable solution for increasing food production efficiency while reducing land use and mitigating environmental uncertainties. However, maintaining optimal lighting conditions remains a challenge due to the complete reliance on artificial light sources. This study proposes a Hybrid RL-PID (Reinforcement Learning - Proportional-Integral-Derivative) control approach for intelligent lighting regulation in an indoor controlled environment agriculture (CEA) system. The hybrid method integrates the fast response of PID controllers with the adaptive learning capabilities of reinforcement learning to optimize energy consumption while ensuring ideal light intensity for plant growth. Simulation results indicate that the proposed Hybrid RL-PID achieved 22% energy savings compared to conventional PID control, reduced oscillations by 50% through moving average filtering, and stabilized light intensity in 15 iterations without overshooting. The findings highlight the potential of Hybrid RL-PID as a superior lighting control solution for smart farming applications and energy-efficient CEA systems.

title: Pushing Bit-Width Limits in LLM Quantization with Saliency-Guided Mix-Precision Allocation and Learnable Affine Transformation
authors: Shuoyu Ma, Wenrui Dai, Maida Cao, Shaohui Li, Ziyang Zheng, Chenglin Li, Junni Zou, Hongkai Xiong
publish: 2026 Data Compression Conference (DCC)
abstract: Despite strong generalization ability, large language models (LLMs) are resource-intensive to deploy. Post-training quantization (PTQ) effectively compresses pretrained LLMs without retraining. Although recent studies target extremely low-bit quantization, most methods rely on extra parameters and the effective bit-width usually exceeds 2 bits. We introduce a saliency-guided mixed-precision PTQ framework that achieves an average bit-width of 1.70 bits while maintaining competitive performance. The proposed method allocates bit-widths across weight groups under a strict total budget, guided by a cross-precision saliency metric. The key innovation is a learnable affine transformation consisting of rotation, scaling, and shifting to align quantization grids with local weight distributions to minimize error. Experiments show that the proposed method matches state-of-the-art low-bit PTQ performance, while reducing the average bit-width by approximately 0.55 bits in Table 1.

title: Sparse2Dense: A Keypoint-Driven Generative Framework for Human Video Compression and Vertex Prediction
authors: Bolin Chen, Ru-Ling Liao, Yan Ye, Jie Chen, Shanzhi Yin, Xinrui Ju, Shiqi Wang, Yibo Fan
publish: 2026 Data Compression Conference (DCC)
abstract: For bandwidth-constrained multimedia applications, simultaneously achieving ultra-low bitrate human video compression and accurate vertex prediction remains a critical challenge, as it demands the harmonization of dynamic motion modeling, detailed appearance synthesis, and geometric consistency. To address this challenge, we propose Sparse2Dense, a keypoint-driven generative framework that leverages extremely sparse 3D keypoints as compact transmitted symbols to enable ultra-low bitrate human video compression and precise human vertex prediction. The key innovation is the multi-task learning-based and keypointaware deep generative model, which could encode complex human motion via compact 3D keypoints and leverage these sparse keypoints to estimate dense motion for video synthesis with temporal coherence and realistic textures. Additionally, a vertex predictor is integrated to learn human vertex geometry through joint optimization with video generation, ensuring alignment between visual content and geometric structure. Extensive experiments demonstrate that the proposed Sparse2Dense framework achieves competitive compression performance for human video over traditional/generative video codecs, whilst enabling precise human vertex prediction for downstream geometry applications. As such, Sparse2Dense is expected to facilitate bandwidth-efficient human-centric media transmission, such as realtime motion analysis, virtual human animation, and immersive entertainment.

title: A 28nm 11.2-to-20-GHz PLL-based 16-Phase Clock Generator with High-Order-Mode Ring Oscillator Achieving 24.3-fs Jitter for 320-Gsps-Sample-Rate Data Converter
authors: Zonglin Ye, Longxiang Hou, Yuxuan Sun, Yuhan Ding, Yixuan Wen, Hongyang Zhang, Hao Luo, Yi Yang, Chenming Zhang, Xinlin Geng, Qian Xie, Cheng Wang, Jun Zhou, Zheng Wang
publish: 2026 IEEE Custom Integrated Circuits Conference (CICC)
abstract: This work proposes a PLL-based multi-phase clock generator with a high-order-mode ring oscillator (HOMRO) that achieves high frequency and multi-phase capability simultaneously and an ultra-low-noise sampling loop. The proposed MPCG is measured to achieve 24.3-fs jitter and generate 20-GHz 16-phase clock with 440-fs skew. The tuning range of the MPCG is across 11.2 to 20 GHz, with jitter variation of ±6.3 fs and skew variation of ±60 fs.

title: Rapid Diagnosis of Foot-and-Mouth Disease in Livestock Using Supervised Machine Learning Algorithms
authors: Ebtsam Mahmmod, Almabruk Sultan
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Maghreb Meeting of the Conference on Sciences and Techniques of Automatic Control and Computer Engineering (MI-STA)
abstract: Foot and mouth disease (FMD) is considered a major cause of economic losses in many countries, and early diagnosis is crucial to minimizing these losses. This research proposes a novel approach for early FMD diagnosis using a supervised learning algorithm. The main objective is to develop an FMD diagnosis model using a preprocessing approach and selecting important features with an Extra Tree. The cattle symptom dataset, a freely available and publicly accessible resource, was utilized, and its application was examined using the Random Forest (RF) and Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost) algorithms. After comparing the two, the XGBoost algorithm achieved the highest accuracy, reaching 100% in FMD diagnosis. This demonstrates that machine learning (ML) can be relied upon to develop more effective methods for FMD diagnosis, assisting veterinarians in early disease diagnosis and preserving livestock.

title: Distance Enhancement and Misalignment Robustness in WPT System Employing Higher Order Compensation Topology and ZVS
authors: Praduman Amroliya, S. K. Sharma
publish: 2026 IEEE Madhya Pradesh Section Conference (MPCON)
abstract: Wireless power transfer (WPT) related issues such as distance optimization, misalignment resilience and zero voltage switching (ZVS) can be handled by higher order compensation. The proposed work aims to improve the effectiveness of wireless power transfer (WPT) systems. The study explores the function of compensation design in maximizing mutual inductance and guaranteeing effective power transmission over long distances utilizing a MATLABbased modeling technique. To preserve steady ZVS functioning and lessen the negative consequences of coil misalignment— in real-world WPT applications—advanced compensation topologies are examined. The simulation findings show that high-order correction increases the resilience of WPT systems over varying operating circumstances in addition to increase efficiency and dependability. The quality factor (Q) and the coupling coefficient (k) of the system is calculated as 42.73 and 0.9 respectively, which ensure optimum power transfer efficiency of the system. The proposed MATLAB model design is a potential option for high-performance WPT applications including industrial automation and electric car charging as it achieves dependable long-distance operation and resilience against misalignment.

title: A 500kHz 600V Capacitive Isolated SiC Converter with Switching-On Transition Detection for Hard-Switching Fault Prognosis Achieving 58ns Short-Circuit Protection
authors: Qing Yuan, Yingping Chen, Ming Liu
publish: 2026 IEEE Custom Integrated Circuits Conference (CICC)
abstract: Focusing on silicon-carbide (SiC) reliability, a capacitive isolated SiC power system is designed for 500kHz 600V power processing. A switching-on transition detector is developed to achieve fast hard-switching fault (HSF) prognosis. In addition, a tri-state H-bridge gate driver is fully integrated to avoid false conduction of the low-side SiC power transistor, mitigating the crosstalk under high-speed and high-voltage switching operation. Fabricated in a 0.35μm BCD process, the design realizes an HSF detection within 45.5ns and short-circuit protection within 58ns, improved by at least 4 times. The Miller coupling effect successfully removed under a voltage slew rate of 125V/ns, while reducing the area of the fully integrated tri-state gate driver by 2.7 times.

title: TOKCIM: A 65nm All-Digital Compute-in-Memory Transformer Accelerator with Advanced Weight Sparsity Handling and SoftMax Acceleration
authors: Shaoxuan Li, Zikun Wei, Ruihao He, Cong Wang, Ye Gu, Tianchu Dong, Yucheng Cai, Hongwu Jiang
publish: 2026 IEEE Custom Integrated Circuits Conference (CICC)
abstract: This paper presents TOKCIM, a Transformer accelerator based on digital compute-in-memory (DCIM) structure. TOKCIM adopts all-zero initialization (AZI) characteristics in the DCIM macros, together with group pruning of weights to fully leverage the benefits of AZI, speeding up both weight loading process and in-memory computing process. The SoftMax unit (SMU) in TOKCIM employs an optimal value selection strategy. By selecting several optimal values among inputs and performing SoftMax operations only on the selected values, the proposed SMU can significantly reduce the overhead of activation calculations with negligible degradation on performance. Fabricated in 65-nm CMOS process and benchmarked on Q8BERT model and GLUE dataset, TOKCIM achieves a measured energy efficiency of 5.73 TOPS/W and a compute efficiency of 0.29 TOPS/mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>, demonstrating state-of-the-art performance.

title: A 10-bit 20-GS/s 4-Channel Time-Interleaved DAC Employing Hybrid-Segmentation in 28-nm CMOS
authors: Yunjie Chen, Duran Ckristian, Koji Asami, Sota Kano, Ritaro Takenaka, Zolboo Byambadorj, Masahiro Ishida, Akio Higo, Tetsuya Iizuka
publish: 2026 IEEE Custom Integrated Circuits Conference (CICC)
abstract: This paper proposes a four-channel (4-ch) time-interleaved (TI) high-speed current-steering Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC) with hybrid segmentation that employs a single-stage analog multiplexer followed by a current summation. Compared with conventional 4-ch structures, the proposed architecture simplifies the clocking scheme by reducing the number of required clocks and eliminating the need for high-speed clock signals. In addition, the proposed design does not have the dead-zone in the middle of the bandwidth and reduces the sensitivity to duty-cycle mismatches. The DAC was fabricated in 28 nm CMOS, incorporating a simple differential clock timing calibration to ensure precise synchronization of sub-DACs. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed 10-bit, 20-GS/s DAC achieves >40 dBc spurious-free dynamic range (SFDR) for a signal frequency up to 6.3 GHz, representing an improvement over state-of-the-art prior 4-ch time-interleaved DACs

title: Detection and Classification of Abnormal Human Actions for Video Surveillance on Edge Devices
authors: Jonathan Flores Monroy, Gibran Benitez Garcia, Mariko Nakano Miyatake, Hiroki Takahashi
publish: 2025 IEEE 43rd Central America and Panama Convention (CONCAPAN XLIII)
abstract: The increase in violence in urban areas has created an urgent need for systems capable of accurately identifying actions that endanger citizens. In this context, video surveillance systems supported by video anomaly detection approaches have served as a foundation for developing automatic solutions to identify unusual behaviors; however, most of these methods focus exclusively on detection and do not address the detailed classification of anomalies. This work proposes a modular framework for the joint detection and classification of Abnormal Human Actions, understood as anomalous actions caused by humans that pose a threat to public safety. The proposed approach, evaluated on an edge device, achieved an accuracy of $59.02 \%$ in Abnormal Human Actions classification, a detection rate of $80.0 \%$ on the UCF-Crime dataset, and a processing speed of 283.49 fps, demonstrating its potential for deployment in real-world, resource-constrained video surveillance environments.
